data.title: Pantry cleaning: Berkshire dumps a sour bet as Kraft Heinz splits the ketchup
data.description:
The Kraft Heinz Company (KHC) fell sharply in premarket action after filing paperwork with the SEC that indicated Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) is likely to sell all or parts of its massive block of shares. The development was not a complete surprise after former CEO Warren Buffett said he was disappointed in Kraft's (KHC) ambitious breakup plan. Notably, a sale of the KHC block would be Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) CEO Greg Abel's first major move since taking over.

Last year, Kraft Heinz (KHC) announced the major restructuring plan to split into two independent, publicly traded businesses by the second half of 2026. The decision arrived after a period of disappointing financial results, ongoing strategic review, and pressure to reignite growth across its broad consumer packaged goods portfolio.

The separation is expected to allow management of each business to realize more efficient capital allocation and streamlined operations. The spinoff will be structured as a tax-free transaction for shareholders.

Following the spinoff, Global Taste Elevation Co. will comprise iconic brands like Heinz, Philadelphia cream cheese, and Kraft Mac & Cheese, with over $15 billion in annualized sales. The division will focus on global sauces, spreads, seasonings, and shelf-stable meals, targeting international markets and food service. Meanwhile, North American Grocery Co. will include brands such as Oscar Mayer, Kraft Singles, and Lunchables, with $10.4 billion in 2024 net sales, concentrating on leading North American grocery categories.

Analysts think both new entities will need to prove they can grow and sustain their iconic brands independently in a rapidly evolving food sector.

Shares of Kraft Heinz (KHC) fell 5.7% in premarket action on Wednesday to $22.42 vs. the 52-week range of $22.91 to $33.35.

data.title: Lam Research in focus as Deutsche Bank ups target ahead of earnings
data.description:
• Lam Research (LRCX) was in focus on Wednesday as Deutsche Bank reiterated its Buy rating and upped its price target on the semiconductor equipment maker ahead of its upcoming earnings report, set to be released on Jan. 28.
  • Shares were fractionally higher in premarket trading.
  • “Sentiment on the semicap group has been very positive thus far in 2026, with companies in our coverage up +25% YTD,” analyst Melissa Weathers wrote in a note to clients. “Fundamental data points across the sector have vastly improved in recent months, which we believe warrants updates to our estimates and our valuation framework. As such, with this note we are raising revenue/EPS estimates +5%/+8% for CY26, and +9%/+14% for CY27. As a result, we now forecast LRCX solidly above Street in the near-term (our revenue estimates stand +2%/+5% above Consensus for Dec-qtr/Mar-qtr), and more importantly, now see CY26 rev/EPS [estimates] +7%/+11% vs. the Street, and CY27 rev/EPS [estimates] +10%/+19% above the Street.”
  • Weathers upped her price target on Lam Research to $260 from $195.
  • A consensus of analysts expects Lam Research to earn $1.17 per share on $5.24B in revenue for its fiscal second quarter.

data.title: Gibraltar trades lower after revising down earnings estimates
data.description:
Gibraltar Industries (ROCK) was trading lower after the residential, agtech, and infrastructure solutions provider revised down its earnings estimates.

Shares were -4.09% pre-market to $52.50.

For the three months ended December 31, 2025, net sales guidance has been lowered to $261M-$271M from $283M-$308M. Net sales had come in at $232M in the fourth quarter of 2024.

The consensus revenue estimate for Q4 2025 is $287.00M.

Adjusted EPS guidance has been revised to $0.72-$0.77 from $1.04-$1.14, against the year-ago $0.89 and consensus of $1.02.

For the twelve months ended December 31, 2025, the net sales outlook has been revised down to $1.13B-$1.14B from $1.15B-$1.18B.

Revenue had come in at $1.02B in 2024. The 2025 consensus revenue estimate is $1.17B.

2025 adjusted EPS is estimated at $3.88-$3.93, vs. the prior estimate of $4.20-$4.30 and the consensus estimate of $4.20.

Comparatively, the 2024 adjusted EPS stood at $3.82.

data.title: Super Group declares $0.25 dividend
data.description:
• Super Group (SGHC) declared $0.25/share special dividend.
  • Payable Feb. 9; for shareholders of record Feb. 2; ex-div Feb. 2.
  • See SGHC Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Ambiq Micro announces public offering of 2.2M shares of common stock
data.description:
• Ambiq Micro (AMBQ) announced on Wednesday the commencement of an underwritten public offering of 2.2M shares of its common stock, which includes 2.157M shares of common stock offered by Ambiq and 42,949 shares of common stock offered by certain of Ambiq’s stockholders.
  • In addition, Ambiq expects to grant the underwriters an option for a period of 30 days to purchase up to an additional 330,000 shares of common stock at the public offering price, less underwriting discounts and commissions. 
  • BofA Securities and UBS Investment Bank are acting as joint lead book-running managers for the proposed offering. Needham & Company and Stifel are acting as joint book-running managers for the proposed offering.
  • AMBQ -1.48% premarket to $32.53.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Lodestar Metals announces $1.5M private placement financing
data.description:
• Lodestar Metals Corp (LSTR:CA) (SVTNF) announced a non-brokered private placement financing of 7,500,000 units of the company at a price of $0.20 per unit for gross proceeds of $1,500,000.
  • Each unit under the offering will consist of one common share and one common share purchase warrant, with each warrant entitling the holder to purchase one additional share at a price of $0.30 per share for a period of 18 months from the date of issue, the company said.

data.title: Sovereign AI aligns with Accenture, Palantir to build AI data centers in EMEA
data.description:
U.K.-based AI infrastructure provider Sovereign AI said on Wednesday that it has teamed with Accenture (ACN) and Palantir Technologies (PLTR) to help build its next-generation data centers throughout Europe, the Middle East and Africa.

Accenture shares were fractionally higher in premarket trading, while Palantir shares fell 1.8%.

Also known as S-AI, the U.K. company said the deal will help accelerate the development and deployment of its Dell (DELL) AI Factory and Nvidia (NVDA)-powered capabilities. The data centers are capable of supporting AI workloads and help secure high-performing operations for regulated industries, the company added in a statement.

Palantir will provide its operating system for AI infrastructure, known as Chain Reaction, while Accenture will aid with the digital transformation, operational support, and delivery. 

“Palantir Chain Reaction software is a key advantage in the buildout of AI infrastructure—not only as an operating system for business expansion, but also for delivering enterprise AI capabilities to key verticals,” said Kevin Kawasaki, global head of business development at Palantir.

“Digital and economic security now require sovereign AI designed for efficient inference,” Nvidia executive Justin Boitano added. “S-AI’s collaboration with Accenture, Dell Technologies, Palantir, and NVIDIA sets the blueprint for deploying AI factories engineered to generate tokens at scale—and turn efficient inference directly into revenue.”

S-AI added that it eventually plans to bring its AI data centers to the Asia Pacific region as well.

data.title: Infinity Natural Resources to buy Pennsylvania gas asset in $36M deal
data.description:
Infinity Natural Resources (INR) +3.9% pre-market Wednesday after saying it agreed to acquire Chase Oil's working interest in Infinity's South Bend field in Pennsylvania for ~$36M in stock.

The acquired asset includes 18 producing wells that generated 14 million cf/day of net natural gas production in December, with three additional wells in progress expected to be turned into sales in H1 2026, and 40 additional gross Marcellus locations and 38 gross Utica locations for potential future development.

The bolt-on acquisition follows Infinity's (INR) pending $1.2B Antero Ohio transaction announced last month.

The acquisition "allows us to use our equity currency for the first time to consolidate our core dry gas Pennsylvania position and execute our post-IPO strategy of strategic consolidation within the Appalachian Basin," Infinity (INR) President and CEO Zack Arnold said.

data.title: Truist Financial Q4 earnings beat, revenue misses; issues soft guidance
data.description:
Truist Financial (TFC) slid 2.1% in Wednesday premarket trading after its Q4 revenue fell short of Wall Street expectations and its revenue guidance indicates that 2026 consensus estimates may be set too high. The top line miss consisted of softer-than-expected growth in both net interest income and noninterest income, such as fees. Meanwhile, it plans to boost its share repurchases in Q1.

The bank sees 2026 adjusted revenue (taxable equivalent) rising 4%-5% from $20.5B in 2025. That implies 2026 adjusted revenue of $21.3B-$21.5B (midpoint $21.4B), vs. the average analyst estimate of $21.5B. Net interest income is expected to rise 3%-4% in 2026 from 2025's $14.6B, implying $15.1B-$15.2B vs. the Visible Alpha consensus of $15.1B.

Adjusted expenses for the year are expected to increase 1.25%-2.25% from 2025’s $12.1B, implying $12.3B-$12.4B, vs. Visible Alpha consensus of $12.1B.

For Q1, it expects adjusted revenue to fall 2%-3% from Q4’s $5.3B, implying $5.1B-$5.2B (vs. $5.2B consensus). Adjusted expenses are projected to drop 4%-5% from Q4’s $3.2B.

The lender expects to buy back up to ~$1B of its shares in Q1, vs. $750M it repurchased in Q4.

Q4 adjusted EPS of $1.12, vs. the $1.09 consensus, rose from $1.04 in Q3 and $0.91 in Q4 2024.

Q4 adjusted revenue of $5.30B, trailing the $5.32B consensus, grew from $5.24B in the prior quarter and $5.11B a year ago.

Net interest income (“TE”) of $3.75B, missing the Visible Alpha consensus of $3.76B, declined from $3.68B in Q3 and $3.64B in the prior year's Q4.

Noninterest income of $1.55B (vs. the Visible Alpha estimate of $1.56B) edged down from $1.56B in the prior quarter and increased from $1.47B a year ago.

Noninterest expense of $3.17B climbed from $3.01B in Q3 and $3.04B a year ago, reflecting higher other expense and higher personnel expense, partially offset by lower regulatory costs.

Provision for credit losses was $512M, higher than the $494M Visible Alpha estimate and increasing from $436M in Q3 and $471M in Q4 2024.

Truist’s (TFC) preprovision net revenue of $2.13B fell from $2.22B in Q3 and $2.08B in Q4 2024.

Average total deposits of $396B slipped from $397B in Q3. Average total loans and leases were $325B, vs. $320B in the prior quarter.

Conference call at 8:00 AM ET.
